blocks.text: Sparky lost three days of Unity work to an auto-update reboot. Three days. That is half a sprint. The machine was not set up as a production environment — it was set up like a home PC. In a studio, every machine has a role: development machines have controlled update schedules, production rendering machines do not receive updates during active projects, and test machines receive all updates first. This is not overcomplicated — it is a one-time configuration that saves you weeks of pain across a year. Set it up once, document it in the studio wiki, and move on. The machine should be working for the project, not against it.

blocks.title: Python Virtual Environment — Standard Project Setup
blocks.defaultCode:
# Step 1: Create a virtual environment in the project folder
python -m venv env

# Step 2: Activate the environment
# Windows:
env\Scripts\activate
# macOS / Linux:
source env/bin/activate

# Step 3: Install project dependencies
pip install pandas matplotlib openpyxl

# Step 4: Freeze the dependency list to a file
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# The requirements.txt file now contains:
# pandas==2.1.4
# matplotlib==3.8.2
# openpyxl==3.1.2
# (plus all their sub-dependencies)

# Step 5: Another developer recreates the exact environment
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Step 6: Deactivate the environment when done
deactivate
blocks.challenge: Sloane's data pipeline uses pandas 1.5.3, but a new project requires pandas 2.1.4. Without virtual environments, installing 2.1.4 breaks the old pipeline. Modify this setup to create two separate environments — `env-legacy` (pandas 1.5.3) and `env-new` (pandas 2.1.4) — and write the command to switch between them on Windows. Then explain in a comment why `pip install -r requirements.txt` is safer than `pip install pandas` for a shared team project.

blocks.title: Studio Software Management Policy Implementation
blocks.software: Windows Settings / Google Sheets / VS Code terminal
blocks.intro: Implement the core software management practices on your own machine and document them for the studio policy.
steps: Open Windows Settings > Windows Update > Advanced Options. Disable 'Receive updates for other Microsoft products.' Set Active Hours to cover your typical work hours so reboots cannot occur during work time. Take a screenshot.
Open VS Code's integrated terminal. Navigate to your Documents folder: cd ~/Documents
Create a new project folder: mkdir rory-mgmt-demo and navigate into it: cd rory-mgmt-demo
Create a Python virtual environment: python -m venv env
Activate it (Windows): env\Scripts\activate — you should see (env) appear at the start of your prompt.
Install two libraries: pip install requests beautifulsoup4
Generate a requirements file: pip freeze > requirements.txt
Open requirements.txt in VS Code and inspect the contents. Note every library listed, including sub-dependencies you did not explicitly install.
Deactivate the environment: deactivate
Open Google Sheets and create a software management register for your own machine: list five applications, their versions, last update date, and whether auto-update is enabled or controlled.

In a new VS Code file called STUDIO-POLICY.md, write three short sections: (1) Update Management Policy, (2) Dependency Management Policy, (3) Backup Policy. Base each on the practices from this lesson. This becomes part of the studio wiki.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · TROUBLESHOOT SLOANE'S ENVIRONMENT
blocks.text:
Sloane's requirements.txt (from her global environment) is a disaster. Here is what it contains:

pandas==1.5.3
pandas==2.1.4
numpy==1.23.0
numpy==1.26.4
requests==2.28.1
openpyxl==3.0.10
flask==2.2.2
flask==3.0.0

Task 1: Identify every conflict in this file. Explain why pip will fail to install from this requirements.txt.

Task 2: Sloane says she needs pandas 2.1.4 for the new dashboard project, but her legacy ETL script requires pandas 1.5.3. Write the complete terminal commands (Windows) to create TWO separate virtual environments that resolve this conflict without either script breaking.

Task 3: Write a three-bullet 'requirements.txt hygiene' rule set for the studio wiki that would prevent this situation from recurring. Each rule should be actionable, not generic.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before the policy document goes to the studio manager:

1. You recommend disabling auto-updates on production machines. A security researcher just published a critical zero-day vulnerability in Windows that is being actively exploited. Does your policy cover this exception? Write the specific policy clause that handles emergency security patches differently from standard feature updates.

2. The 3-2-1 backup rule says one copy must be offsite or in the cloud. Leo stores his design files on his studio workstation and a USB drive he keeps in his desk drawer. How many of the 3-2-1 criteria does this meet, and what specific risk does the current setup leave Leo exposed to?

3. You committed a requirements.txt to the project repository. It includes the `env/` folder in the same commit. Why is committing the `env/` folder itself a problem, and how should it be handled instead?

question.text: Sloane runs `pip install pandas` on her studio machine and it updates pandas from version 1.5.3 to 2.1.4 globally. Her legacy ETL script immediately breaks with an ImportError. What is the correct long-term solution to prevent this class of problem?
options: Avoid updating any Python libraries to maintain stability
Use a separate Python virtual environment for each project, with a requirements.txt file specifying exact versions
Uninstall the new version and reinstall the old version each time she switches projects
Use two separate computers — one for legacy projects and one for new projects
question.explanation: Virtual environments isolate each project's dependencies from the global Python installation and from other projects. A requirements.txt file with pinned version numbers (e.g. pandas==1.5.3) ensures every developer recreates the exact same environment. This is the professional standard. Manually switching versions is error-prone and does not scale. Using separate computers is wasteful and still does not address the underlying configuration management problem.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3004 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 15 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

Sloane has handed you the festival budget tracker and it is a mess. Hardcoded numbers everywhere, formulas that reference cells by address with no named ranges, no data validation, and a 'Total' column that adds a fixed number of rows — which means it silently breaks every time someone adds a new budget line.

The studio uses spreadsheets as mini-applications, not just tables. Clients interact with them directly, which means they must be robust, self-validating, and readable without the original author present. Your sprint task: rebuild the festival budget tracker as a professional-grade spreadsheet using named ranges, structured formulas, conditional formatting, and data validation. Sloane will stress-test it on Friday.
Spreadsheets as Applications: The Y12 Mindset Shift

At Year 12, the shift from 'using a spreadsheet' to 'designing a spreadsheet' requires treating the file as a product. A well-designed spreadsheet separates data entry from calculation from presentation, uses consistent naming conventions, validates inputs before they cause downstream errors, and works correctly even when the user does something unexpected. This is called defensive spreadsheet design, and it is standard practice in finance, operations, and project management.

Named ranges transform a spreadsheet from a grid of cell references into something readable. A formula like `=SUMIF(Category,"Design",Amount)` is immediately understandable; `=SUMIF(B2:B47,"Design",D2:D47)` requires the reader to navigate to those cells to understand what they contain. Named ranges also make formulas resilient to structural changes — if rows are inserted or deleted, named ranges adjust automatically. This is not a comfort feature: it is a correctness feature.

Nested IF functions and lookup formulas (VLOOKUP, XLOOKUP, INDEX/MATCH) are the logic layer of spreadsheet design. They allow a spreadsheet to respond intelligently to input — classifying budget items, flagging overruns, retrieving data from reference tables without manual copying. At Y12 level, you are expected to construct these formulas from first principles, debug them when they return unexpected results, and explain the logic to a non-technical stakeholder.

items.definition: A label assigned to a cell or range of cells (e.g. naming B2:B100 as 'Category'). Named ranges make formulas readable, reduce reference errors, and automatically adjust when rows are inserted or deleted.

A function that searches the first column of a table for a value and returns data from a specified column in the same row. Syntax: =VLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, col_index_num, [range_lookup]). Superseded by XLOOKUP in newer versions of Excel/Sheets.
SUMIF sums values in a range where a corresponding range meets a single condition. SUMIFS supports multiple conditions. Syntax: =SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range).

A spreadsheet feature that automatically changes the appearance (colour, font, border) of cells based on their value or formula result. Used to visually highlight overruns, anomalies, or status indicators.

A spreadsheet feature that restricts what values can be entered into a cell — for example, a dropdown list of approved categories, or a rule that only allows numbers between 0 and 100,000. Prevents input errors at the source.

A relative reference (e.g. A1) adjusts when a formula is copied to another cell. An absolute reference (e.g. $A$1) does not adjust. Mixed references ($A1 or A$1) lock one dimension only. Critical to understand for formulas that are dragged across ranges.

blocks.title: Advanced Spreadsheet Formulas — Festival Budget Tracker
blocks.challenge: The studio manager wants to see the percentage of the total budget consumed by each category. Using the named ranges above, write a formula for a 'Budget Share %' column that calculates each category's Budgeted amount as a percentage of the grand total. Use an absolute reference for the total so the formula can be dragged down without breaking. Then write a SUMIFS formula that totals Actual spend ONLY for items where Category is 'Design' AND Status is 'Over Budget'.

rows: Sums values where one condition is met
Sums where multiple conditions are all met
Forgetting that sum_range comes FIRST in SUMIFS (opposite to COUNTIFS)
Returns value from a table at a specified column
Using TRUE (approximate match) when exact match is needed; table not sorted
Modern replacement for VLOOKUP; searches any column
Not available in Excel 2016 or earlier
Returns different values based on multiple conditions
Exceeding 7 nested levels; forgetting closing parentheses
Cell reference does not shift when formula is copied
Forgetting $ signs when a formula is dragged across rows/columns

blocks.title: Rebuild the Festival Budget Tracker
blocks.intro: You will build a professional-grade budget tracker from scratch using the design principles and formulas covered in this lesson.
steps: Open a new Google Sheets (or Excel) file. Name it 'Festival Budget Tracker v2 — Bunbury Tech Launchpad'.
Create a 'Data' sheet tab. Add these column headers in row 1: Item ID | Category | Description | Budgeted ($) | Actual ($) | Variance ($) | Status | Supplier Code.
Add at least 10 rows of sample festival budget data across four categories: Design, Logistics, Technology, Marketing.

Select column B (Category). Go to Data > Data Validation. Set the validation type to 'List of items' and enter: Design,Logistics,Technology,Marketing. This creates a dropdown and prevents invalid input.
In column F (Variance), enter the formula =D2-E2 for the first data row. Do NOT hardcode numbers. Copy this formula down all data rows.
In column G (Status), enter the nested IF formula from the codePreview block. Adjust the thresholds if needed for your data. Copy down.
Select column G. Apply conditional formatting: red fill for 'Over Budget,' orange fill for 'At Risk,' green fill for 'On Track.'
Select columns B through H (the data range). Go to Data > Named Ranges (Sheets) or Formulas > Name Manager (Excel). Define the named ranges: Category, Description, Budgeted, Actual, Variance, Status.
Create a second sheet tab called 'Summary.' Add a category breakdown table with SUMIF formulas for Budgeted and Actual amounts for each of the four categories.
Add a 'Grand Total' row at the bottom of the summary using SUM formulas. Add a 'Budget Used %' row: =SUM(Actual)/SUM(Budgeted) formatted as a percentage.
Test the data validation: attempt to type an invalid category in column B. Confirm the dropdown appears and rejects invalid text.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SPREADSHEET STRESS TEST
blocks.text:
Sloane is stress-testing your budget tracker. Here are the four things she is going to try:

Test 1 — She inserts a new row in the middle of the data range. Do your SUMIF formulas still capture the new row? Do your named ranges expand to include it? (Hint: named ranges in Google Sheets do NOT automatically expand when rows are inserted inside them.)

Test 2 — She types 'design' (lowercase) in the Category column. Does your SUMIF pick it up, or is it case-sensitive? (Google Sheets SUMIF is not case-sensitive; Excel is. How does this affect your formula design?)

Test 3 — She enters a negative number in the Budgeted column. Your Status formula uses 'Over Budget' when Variance < -500. What does a negative budget create as output, and how would you add input validation to prevent it?

Test 4 — She wants to add a 'Q1 Budget' and 'Q2 Budget' split. You currently have a single Budgeted column. What structural change to the spreadsheet would support quarterly tracking without breaking existing formulas?

Answer each test as a written troubleshooting note.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before Sloane gets this tracker:

1. Your VLOOKUP formula references Sheet2!$A:$C — the entire columns A through C. On a spreadsheet with 10,000 rows of supplier data, how does searching an entire column range affect spreadsheet performance compared to referencing a defined table range like Sheet2!$A$1:$C$500? What feature in Excel Tables (Ctrl+T) would make this reference both readable and dynamically sized?

2. You used a nested IF with three levels to produce 'On Track / At Risk / Over Budget.' The studio manager now wants five status levels. At what point does a nested IF become unmanageable, and what alternative formula approach (using a lookup table) would produce the same result while being easier to maintain?

3. The original tracker had hardcoded totals that silently broke when rows were added. You fixed this with named ranges and SUMIF. But a junior team member could still break it by accidentally deleting the named range definition. What two protective measures can you apply to the spreadsheet to prevent accidental modification of the formula infrastructure?

question.text: A spreadsheet formula in cell G2 is: =SUMIF(Category, "Design", Budgeted). When this formula is copied to cell G3, what happens?
options: The formula breaks because named ranges cannot be used in SUMIF
The formula in G3 becomes =SUMIF(Category, "Design", Budgeted) — identical, because named ranges do not shift when copied
The formula adjusts to =SUMIF(Category, "Marketing", Budgeted) because the criteria argument shifts down one row
The formula returns an error because the criteria must be a cell reference, not a text string
question.explanation: Named ranges behave like absolute references — they refer to the same cells regardless of where the formula is copied. When you copy =SUMIF(Category, "Design", Budgeted) to any cell, it continues to reference the same named ranges and produces the same result. This is one of the key advantages of named ranges over cell address references, where SUMIF(B2:B100, "Design", D2:D100) would shift incorrectly when copied.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3005 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 URGENT | Sprint 16 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

Sloane has pulled 1,847 rows of festival ticket sales data from the event platform. The client wants answers by 9 AM tomorrow: which ticket category generated the highest revenue, which day of the week had peak sales, and which postcode cluster is underperforming. The data is raw — inconsistent capitalisation in the Category column, blank rows, duplicate transaction IDs, and dates stored as text strings.

Your task: clean the dataset, build pivot tables answering the three questions, and produce a one-page executive summary with supporting charts. Sloane says if you sort without filtering duplicates first, your pivot will lie to you. She is correct. Work systematically — data cleaning before analysis, always.
Data Analysis Workflow: Clean First, Analyse Second

Data analysis in a professional context follows a strict sequence: data cleaning, then transformation, then analysis, then visualisation. Skipping or reordering steps is the fastest way to produce confidently wrong answers. A pivot table built on dirty data — duplicates, inconsistent categories, blank rows — produces numbers that look authoritative but are misleading. The client makes a decision based on those numbers, and when the error is discovered, the trust you spent months building is gone in one meeting.

Sorting and filtering are not just navigational tools — they are analytical techniques. Multi-level sorting (sort by Category, then by Date, then by Revenue) reveals patterns invisible in unsorted data. Advanced filter criteria with AND/OR logic allow you to isolate specific subsets for comparison. At Y12 level, you are expected to use these tools intentionally, not randomly, and to document your analytical decisions so another team member can reproduce your results.

Pivot tables are the most powerful analytical tool available in a spreadsheet application, and most users use approximately 20% of their capability. A pivot table does not just summarise data — it allows you to dynamically reframe the same dataset from multiple analytical angles without writing a single formula. The key skill is choosing the right field for each pivot zone (Rows, Columns, Values, Filters) based on the analytical question you are answering, not based on how the data happens to be organised.

items.definition: An interactive data summarisation tool that aggregates, groups, and cross-tabulates large datasets without modifying the source data. Allows dynamic reorganisation of analysis by dragging fields between Rows, Columns, Values, and Filters.

The process of identifying and correcting errors, inconsistencies, and missing values in a dataset before analysis. Includes removing duplicates, standardising formats, handling blanks, and correcting data types.
TRIM removes leading, trailing, and extra internal spaces from text. CLEAN removes non-printable characters. Both are essential for standardising imported data before analysis or matching.
A sort operation that orders data by multiple columns in sequence. For example: sort by Category A→Z, then within each category sort by Revenue largest→smallest.

A filtering mode (available in Excel) that allows complex filter criteria — including AND/OR combinations and wildcard matching — defined in a separate criteria range rather than through dropdown menus.
Summary measures that describe a dataset's central tendency and spread: mean (average), median (middle value), mode (most frequent value), minimum, maximum, and standard deviation.

rows: Sort by transaction ID; visually scan for repeats
Select data range > Data > Remove Duplicates (Excel) or Data > Data Cleanup > Remove Duplicates (Sheets)
Sort the Category column and scan visually
Add a helper column with =PROPER(B2) or =UPPER(B2); paste-special as values to replace originals
Dates left-align instead of right-align; sort does not work chronologically
Use =DATEVALUE(A2) to convert; or Text to Columns wizard in Excel
VLOOKUP returns no match despite apparent identical values
Apply =TRIM(A2) in a helper column; paste-special as values
Pivot table includes a blank category; counts are off
Filter for blanks (filter > sort > blank check); delete blank rows; or use Go To Special > Blanks
Select range > Data > Text to Columns > Finish (forces conversion); or multiply by 1

blocks.title: Data Analysis Formulas — Festival Ticket Sales
blocks.challenge: Sloane needs a formula that calculates the average revenue per transaction for each Category, without using a pivot table — purely using worksheet formulas. Write an AVERAGEIF formula to do this. Then write a second formula that identifies the category with the HIGHEST average transaction revenue, using a combination of AVERAGEIF and MATCH (or INDEX/MATCH). Explain in a comment why you would still prefer a pivot table for this task in a production context.

blocks.title: Festival Ticket Sales Analysis
blocks.intro: Work through the complete data analysis pipeline on the provided ticket sales dataset. Follow the clean-transform-analyse-visualise sequence.
steps: Open the provided festival-tickets-raw.xlsx file (or create a sample dataset with 20+ rows: columns TransactionID, Date, Category, Quantity, UnitPrice, Revenue, Postcode).
Data cleaning step: Use COUNTIF to flag duplicate TransactionIDs in a helper column. Formula: =COUNTIF($A$2:$A$2000,A2)>1. Filter for TRUE values and delete duplicate rows.
Standardise the Category column: add a helper column with =PROPER(TRIM(B2)). Copy the helper column, paste-special as Values only over the original Category column, then delete the helper column.
Check the Date column: if dates are left-aligned, they are stored as text. Add a helper column with =DATEVALUE(C2), format as Date, paste-special as Values to replace the text dates.
Add a DayOfWeek column: =TEXT(C2, "dddd") to extract the day name from each date.
Insert a Pivot Table (Insert > PivotTable). Place it on a new sheet called 'Analysis.'
Pivot Table 1 — Revenue by Category: drag Category to Rows, Revenue to Values (Sum). Sort by Sum of Revenue descending.
Pivot Table 2 — Revenue by Day of Week: drag DayOfWeek to Rows, Revenue to Values (Sum), Quantity to Values (Sum). Identify peak day.
Pivot Table 3 — Revenue by Postcode: drag Postcode to Rows, Revenue to Values (Sum). Sort descending. Identify the bottom 5 postcodes.
Create a bar chart from Pivot Table 1 and a line chart from Pivot Table 2. Label axes, add a title, and remove chart junk (unnecessary gridlines, legend if redundant).
On a new sheet called 'Executive Summary,' write three bullet points answering the client's three questions, citing the specific numbers from your pivot tables.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SLOANE'S DATA QUALITY CHALLENGE
blocks.text:
Sloane has sent you a second dataset: postcode-performance.csv. It has three problems she wants you to diagnose and fix before the client presentation.

Problem 1: The Revenue column contains values formatted as '$1,234.56' (text with dollar sign and comma). When you SUM the column it returns 0. Write the Excel formula that extracts the numeric value from this text string. (Hint: you will need SUBSTITUTE and VALUE.)

Problem 2: The Postcode column contains a mix of 4-digit postcodes (correct: '6230') and 3-digit postcodes (likely missing a leading zero: '623' instead of '0623'). Write a formula that detects postcodes shorter than 4 characters and displays the corrected version with a leading zero. (Hint: LEN and TEXT functions.)

Problem 3: The Category column has three spellings of the same thing: 'Gen Admission', 'General Admission', and 'GA'. Sloane wants all three standardised to 'General Admission'. Write a formula using nested SUBSTITUTE or IFS that normalises all three spellings. Show the formula logic step by step.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before the executive summary goes to the client:

1. Your pivot table shows Saturday as the highest revenue day. But Sloane points out that there were three Saturdays in the dataset and only one Friday. Is total revenue the right metric to compare day-of-week performance, or should you be using average revenue per occurrence? What change to the pivot table's Value Field Settings would give a fairer comparison?

2. You reported 'Postcode 6230 is underperforming.' The dataset has 847 transactions in postcode 6230 and only 12 in postcode 6232. Is 'underperforming' a meaningful comparison when the transaction counts are so different? What additional metric would make this comparison valid?

3. The client wants to filter the pivot table to show only transactions above $100 revenue. You applied a manual filter on the source data. Why is filtering the source data the wrong approach here, and what pivot-table-specific tool should you use instead?

question.text: Sloane runs =SUM(D2:D1848) on a Revenue column and gets 0, even though the column appears to contain numbers. What is the most likely cause?
options: The SUM formula has the wrong range — it should reference the entire column
The numbers in column D are stored as text (e.g. imported with a dollar sign or comma), so SUM treats them as non-numeric
Google Sheets and Excel use different SUM syntax, causing the formula to fail
The column has conditional formatting applied, which interferes with the SUM calculation
question.explanation: When numbers are stored as text — for example, imported as '$1,234.56' or '1,234' with a comma — Excel and Sheets cannot perform arithmetic on them. SUM ignores text values and returns 0. The fix is to use =VALUE(SUBSTITUTE(SUBSTITUTE(D2,"$",""),",","")) to strip the formatting characters and convert to a true number, or use the Text to Columns tool to force conversion. Conditional formatting has no effect on calculated values.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3006 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 SPRINT FINAL | Sprint 17 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

This is the capstone sprint. The Collie River Festival campaign ships on Friday. Every team member's output must be integrated into a single cohesive deliverable: Leo's SVG assets, Sparky's hardware display config, Sloane's analytics dashboard data, and your own spreadsheet models and project documentation.

Integrated digital production means the whole is greater than the sum of the parts — but only if the parts are compatible. You need to manage the handoffs, resolve the format conflicts, ensure version control is active across all assets, and produce a final delivery package that a client can actually open, use, and maintain after we hand it over. Rory's rule: the client does not care about your internal process. They care that everything works on the day.
Integrated Production: Managing Digital Handoffs

Integrated digital production is the discipline of combining outputs from multiple tools, team members, and media types into a unified deliverable. In a professional studio, this means managing asset handoffs (Leo's designs into the web build), format compatibility (Sparky's hardware requirements vs. web delivery requirements), version control (ensuring the team is working from the same source files), and quality assurance (testing the integrated output against the client's actual requirements, not your assumptions about them).

The most common failure point in integrated production is the assumption of compatibility. Leo designed the hero banner at 1920×1080 px, but the CMS requires 1200×630 px for social sharing meta tags. Sparky's LED controller accepts SVG 1.1, but Leo exported SVG 1.2 with features the controller does not support. These are not individual errors — they are coordination failures that a project manager must anticipate, not react to. The fix is a technical specification document agreed before any production begins, not a rush conversion the night before delivery.

Version control is not optional in an integrated production environment. When Sloane updates the data model, Leo's chart assets may need to be re-exported. When Sparky changes the hardware display dimensions, Leo's SVG layout must be updated. Without version-controlled assets and a clear asset dependency map, these cascading changes create chaos. At minimum, every project should use a shared folder with clearly named version suffixes (_v1, _v2, _FINAL, _FINAL-v2 — the last one being a red flag that the versioning discipline has already broken down).

items.definition: The defined sequence of tools and processes through which a raw asset (e.g. a photograph or audio recording) passes to reach its final delivered form. A well-defined pipeline includes format specifications at each stage.

A document agreed before production begins that defines file formats, dimensions, colour profiles, naming conventions, and delivery requirements for all assets. Prevents compatibility failures at handoff.

A system for tracking changes to files over time, allowing the team to identify who changed what, when, and why — and to revert to previous versions if needed. Git is the professional standard for code; shared drives with naming conventions serve this role for design assets.

The transfer of an asset or deliverable from one team member or tool to another. A clean handoff includes the asset in the correct format, at the correct specification, with any necessary documentation about how it was created.

The systematic process of testing a deliverable against its requirements before client delivery. For integrated digital production, QA includes cross-device testing, format validation, link checking, and review against the original brief.

The final set of files delivered to a client, organised so the client can understand, use, and maintain the deliverable independently. Typically includes assets, documentation, source files, and a README explaining the folder structure.

rows: Sparky's LED controller rejects SVG 1.2 features used by Leo
Agree SVG version and feature set in tech spec before production
Re-export SVG from Leo's source file with compatibility settings; test in controller simulator
Hero banner is 1920×1080 but CMS social share requires 1200×630
List all required dimensions per platform in tech spec
Batch export multiple sizes from Leo's master file; do not resize rasterised exports
Leo designs in P3 wide gamut; website renders in sRGB — colours shift noticeably
Specify sRGB as the production colour space in the tech spec
Convert Leo's files to sRGB before export; check in browser
Enforce naming convention at project kickoff: component-variant-version.ext
Audit delivery folder; rename all assets according to convention before packaging
Sloane updates the sales CSV; Leo's chart images are now out of date
Identify asset dependencies at kickoff; flag data-driven assets for update when source data changes
Re-export all data-driven assets from the updated source; version the new exports
Client cannot identify which file is the editable source and which is the export
Include a README in the delivery package explaining every file's purpose and tool required to edit it
Create retrospectively from team knowledge; add to delivery package before handover

blocks.title: Tools for Integrated Digital Production at the Studio

items.label: Version Control: Git + GitHub
points: Industry-standard version control for code, configuration files, and text assets
Every change is logged with author, timestamp, and commit message
Branching allows parallel development without overwriting each other's work
Pull requests enable peer review before changes are merged to main
Not ideal for large binary files (PSD, MP4) — use Git LFS or alternative for media
Rory's requirement: every project repository includes a .gitignore, README, and LICENCE file

items.label: Design Handoff: Canva / Figma
points: Canva: Leo's primary tool — assets can be shared via link with edit or view permissions
Figma: industry standard for UI design handoff — developers can inspect design tokens without needing the app
Both generate shareable links instead of file attachments — single source of truth
Version history built into both tools — earlier versions are recoverable
Export formats configurable per asset: PNG, SVG, PDF, WebP
Limitation: if the link is broken or account access is revoked, the client loses access — always export a backup set

items.label: Asset Organisation: Shared Drive Structure
points: Google Drive / OneDrive: accessible to all team members and the client
Folder structure should mirror the delivery package: /source /exports /delivery
Naming convention enforced at project level: component-variant-vX.ext
Permissions: client gets read-only access to /delivery; team has edit on /source
Not a replacement for Git on code files — use both: Git for code, Drive for media
Backup rule: Drive is synced locally on all team machines — not a single-point-of-failure cloud-only store

blocks.title: Capstone: Build and Package the Festival Campaign Delivery
blocks.software: VS Code / Google Drive / Canva / Google Sheets / Audacity
blocks.intro: You will assemble all sprint outputs into a professional delivery package and perform quality assurance checks before the client handover.
steps: Create a project delivery folder structure in Google Drive: /collie-river-festival /source /exports /delivery /documentation
Move all source files (Leo's Canva designs, Sparky's hardware config, Sloane's CSV data, your spreadsheet models) into /source with correct version naming: component-variant-v1.ext
Produce final exports: export Leo's logo as SVG (for hardware), PNG at 600 dpi (for print), and WebP at 80% quality (for web). Place all exports in /exports.
Perform format QA: open each export in its target tool or browser. Confirm SVG opens correctly in Chrome (drag file into browser tab), PNG dimensions match spec, WebP renders in Chrome and Firefox.

Perform data QA: open Sloane's CSV in Google Sheets. Confirm no blank rows, no duplicate transaction IDs, correct postcode format. Run =COUNTA(A:A)-COUNTBLANK(A:A) to verify row count against the expected total.

Perform audio QA: open the exported voiceover-web.mp3 in Audacity. Confirm sample rate is 44,100 Hz, duration matches the script, and there are no clipping artefacts (peaks above 0 dBFS shown in red).
Create a README.md in VS Code for the delivery package. Include: project name, delivery date, client contact, file inventory (filename, format, purpose, tool needed to edit), and known limitations.
Move all QA-passed assets to /delivery. Confirm the folder contains ONLY delivery-ready files — no source PSDs, no draft versions, no _OLD files.
Share the /delivery folder with the client (Google Drive share link, view only). Test the link in an incognito browser window to confirm client-accessible.

Update the sprint board: mark Ticket #3001 through #3005 as Done, mark #3006 as Ready for Review. Write a one-paragraph sprint retrospective: what integration challenge took the most time, and what technical specification clause would have prevented it.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · PRE-DELIVERY QA CHECKLIST
blocks.text:
Rory has given you the studio's pre-delivery QA checklist. Work through each item for the festival campaign and record Pass / Fail / Not Applicable with a one-line note.

1. All assets in /delivery are in the correct format for their intended use (web, print, hardware). ✓/✗
2. No source or draft files are present in /delivery. ✓/✗
3. File naming follows the convention: component-variant-vX.ext for all assets. ✓/✗
4. README.md is present and lists every file with its purpose and the tool required to edit it. ✓/✗
5. The SVG logo opens and renders correctly in Chrome 120+ without JavaScript errors. ✓/✗
6. The MP3 voiceover plays in a browser audio element (test with a simple HTML file: <audio src='voiceover-web.mp3' controls>). ✓/✗
7. All spreadsheet formulas return expected results with the final production data (not test data). ✓/✗
8. The client's share link is view-only and opens correctly in an incognito browser window. ✓/✗
9. The sprint board has been updated with all ticket statuses and a retrospective note. ✓/✗
10. The studio licence register has been updated to include any new tools used in this sprint. ✓/✗

For any item that fails: write the specific remediation action and the team member responsible.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before this project ships:

1. The README you wrote describes every file in /delivery, but a new team member asks: 'How do I regenerate the festival-revenue-chart.png if the underlying data changes?' The current README does not answer this question. What specific information about the asset's production process should a professional README include for any data-driven asset?

2. You shared the /delivery folder with the client using a Google Drive link set to 'Anyone with the link can view.' The client's marketing manager accidentally shared that link in a public Facebook post. What are the data exposure risks — given the delivery package includes postcode-level customer revenue data — and what permission model should be used for client deliverables containing personal or commercially sensitive data?

3. During sprint retrospective, you identify that the format conflict between Leo's SVG 1.2 export and Sparky's SVG 1.1 controller took four hours to resolve. Write a specific technical specification clause — two or three sentences in plain language — that you would add to the studio's project kickoff template to prevent this exact problem recurring on future projects.

question.text: At the end of a project, the /delivery folder contains the following files: hero-banner-FINAL.png, hero-banner-FINAL-v2.png, hero-banner-FINAL-v2-USE THIS ONE.png, logo.svg, logo_old.svg, voiceover.mp3, voiceover-web.mp3. Which of the following best describes the primary problem with this delivery package?
options: The folder contains both PNG and SVG formats, which is unnecessary duplication
The file naming convention has broken down, making it impossible to identify with confidence which version of each asset is the correct final deliverable
The MP3 file should be in WAV format for client delivery
The delivery package should only contain one file per asset type
question.explanation: The presence of 'FINAL,' 'FINAL-v2,' and 'FINAL-v2-USE THIS ONE' naming patterns indicates that file versioning discipline broke down during the project. A client (or future team member) cannot reliably identify which file to use. Professional practice requires a clear naming convention enforced throughout the project, and a /delivery folder that contains only the confirmed final versions with no ambiguity. Having multiple formats (PNG and SVG) can be entirely appropriate when each serves a different delivery purpose.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4001 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 BLOCKED | Sprint 1 | Assigned to: Junior Project Coordinator

New client landed yesterday — Bunbury Youth Theatre wants a full digital rebrand: new website, promotional video, and a social media content calendar. Leo's standing by with Figma open. Sloane's data schema is drafted. Sparky's network infrastructure is ready.

Problem: nobody has actually READ the client brief yet. I called a meeting and half the team couldn't tell me what the client's actual deadline was or who their target audience is. That is not acceptable. Your job right now is to pull the brief apart, identify every constraint, and give the team a clear picture of what we're building and for whom. Nothing moves until this ticket is closed.
The Brief Is Your Blueprint

A client brief is the single most important document in a digital project. It captures what the client wants, why they want it, who the audience is, what constraints exist, and what success looks like. If you misread the brief — or skip reading it — you are building the wrong thing with the right skills, and that is a complete waste of everyone's time.

At Year 12 level, reading a brief means more than skimming for key words. It means interrogating every claim, flagging ambiguities, and translating vague requests into specific, measurable requirements. The client saying 'we want it to look professional' is not a requirement — it is a starting point for a conversation.

At Bunbury Tech Launchpad we use a structured brief analysis process. Every brief gets broken into six categories before a single task is created: purpose, audience, requirements, constraints, deliverables, and success criteria. If any category is missing from the brief, you go back to the client and ask.

items.definition: A document provided by the client that outlines the purpose, goals, audience, constraints, and expected deliverables for a digital project. It forms the basis of the project scope.

Specific, concrete needs that the final product must meet. Functional requirements describe what the product does; non-functional requirements describe how it performs (speed, accessibility, compatibility).
Limitations that restrict what the project team can do — including budget, timeline, technology platforms, skill levels, and legal obligations.
A tangible output the project must produce — for example, a 60-second promotional video, a five-page website, or an annotated wireframe set.

Measurable standards used to evaluate whether the final product meets the client's goals. For example: 'Website loads in under 3 seconds on mobile' or 'Video achieves 500 views within 30 days of launch'.
A vague or unclear requirement that could be interpreted in multiple ways. Ambiguities must be resolved through client communication before design or development begins.

blocks.text: Anatomy of a Client Brief

headers: Questions to Ask If It's Missing
rows: Why the client needs this digital product
What problem is this product solving? Why now?
Who will use or view the product
Age range? Digital literacy? Device preferences? Location?
What the product must do (features, interactions)
What tasks must users be able to complete?
How well it must perform (speed, accessibility, compatibility)
Is there a fixed deadline? An approved toolchain? A budget cap?
Exactly what files or products must be handed over
Final file formats? Resolution specs? Editable source files needed?
How the client will judge if the project succeeded
How will we know it worked? Any metrics to track post-launch?

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: Every client brief contains at least one time bomb. It might be a legally required accessibility standard they forgot to mention, a platform they assumed you'd use, or a deadline tied to an external event they never told you about. Read the brief three times: once for what's there, once for what's missing, and once for what doesn't make sense. The questions you ask in week one cost nothing. The rework you do in week five costs everything.
Translating Vague Requests Into Measurable Requirements

Clients describe what they want in emotional, subjective language. 'Modern,' 'clean,' 'easy to use,' 'professional' — none of these are requirements. Your job is to translate them into something the team can actually build and test against. This is one of the most valuable skills in project delivery.

blocks.title: Vague Client Language vs Measurable Requirements

items.label: What the Client Said
points: "We want it to look modern and professional"
"It should be easy to use"
"We want it to reach young people"
"The video should be high quality"
"We need it done soon"

items.label: What You Write in the Requirements Doc
points: Design must use sans-serif typography, a two-colour brand palette, and align with provided brand style guide
All primary user tasks completable in 3 steps or fewer; usability tested with 3 representative users
Content optimised for Instagram and TikTok; target audience aged 15–25 in Bunbury region
Exported at 1080p (1920×1080) minimum; colour-graded with consistent white balance
Website live and tested by 15 August; video delivered for review by 1 August

blocks.title: Brief Analysis: Bunbury Youth Theatre
blocks.intro: You've received the Bunbury Youth Theatre client brief. Work through the six-category analysis framework to pull the brief apart before any design or development work starts.
steps: Open a new Google Doc titled 'Brief Analysis — Bunbury Youth Theatre'.
Create a table with the six categories: Purpose, Audience, Requirements, Constraints, Deliverables, Success Criteria.
Read the brief provided by your teacher and extract at least two points for each category into your table.
Highlight in yellow any information that is vague or ambiguous — things that need clarification from the client.
For each highlighted item, write a specific clarifying question in a 'Questions for Client' column.
Identify the single most critical constraint (e.g. fixed deadline, platform requirement) and explain in two sentences why missing this constraint would cause project failure.
Share your analysis with a partner — compare what each of you flagged as ambiguous. Discuss any disagreements.

blocks.title: Scenario: The Missing Constraint
blocks.scenario: The Bunbury Youth Theatre brief states they want 'a new website with information about upcoming shows.' Midway through development, the client reveals that the site must be managed entirely by a 70-year-old volunteer with no technical skills, must work on a server they already own running an outdated CMS, and must meet WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards because they receive government arts funding with accessibility obligations. None of this was in the original brief.

questions.question: Identify THREE constraints that were missing from the original brief. For each constraint, explain specifically how it changes the technical or design approach the team would take.
Describe TWO questions a project coordinator should have asked the client during the brief review stage that would have uncovered these missing constraints.
Evaluate the impact of discovering these constraints mid-development rather than at the start. Use project management terminology in your answer.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · BRIEF AUDIT SPRINT
blocks.text: Rory has dropped three real-world client brief excerpts in the shared drive. Each one has at least two problems — a missing section, an ambiguous requirement, or an unrealistic constraint. Your task: identify every problem in each brief, write a one-sentence fix or clarifying question for each issue, and rank the briefs from 'most ready to proceed' to 'needs full client meeting.' Present your findings in a table. Time limit: 20 minutes. Rory will review your table before the end of the lesson.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Before you submit your brief analysis, check each item:

✅ Every section of the brief has been addressed (no blank rows in your analysis table)
✅ At least three ambiguities have been identified and turned into specific clarifying questions
✅ You have identified the single most critical constraint and explained the consequence of missing it
✅ Your requirements are measurable — no vague language like 'modern' or 'easy to use' without a definition
✅ You've noted which team members (Leo, Sloane, Sparky) are affected by each requirement

If any of these are missing, the brief is not approved and the sprint does not start. Fix it now.

question.text: A client brief states the website should be 'easy for everyone to use.' What is the BEST next step for the project coordinator?
options: Accept the requirement and start designing
Ask the client to define their specific user groups and what tasks they need to complete
Assume 'everyone' means adults aged 18–60 with average digital literacy
Write a non-functional requirement about load speed
question.explanation: Vague requirements must be made measurable before work begins. The phrase 'easy for everyone to use' is too broad — the team needs to know who 'everyone' is and what specific tasks must be achievable to design and test against this requirement.

question.text: A non-functional requirement describes a specific feature or function the digital product must include (e.g. 'the site must have a contact form').
question.explanation: Non-functional requirements describe how the product performs, not what it does. Examples include load time, accessibility compliance, browser compatibility, and uptime. Features like a contact form are functional requirements.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4002 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 2 | Assigned to: Junior Project Coordinator

Brief analysis is approved — nice work. Now the real planning begins. Leo's design is locked to the colour palette in the brief. Sloane's data schema is approved and ready to build against. Sparky's network is ready — the dev environment is live.

What we don't have yet is a project plan. I need a Gantt chart in Trello showing every task from now to delivery, with owners, durations, and dependencies mapped out. If two tasks share a dependency and you haven't shown it, the chart is wrong. I also need a risk register — because this client is launching at the Winter Arts Festival and the date is immovable. Get planning.
From Brief to Plan: Breaking Down the Work

A brief tells you what to build. A project plan tells you how you're going to build it, who will do each piece, and in what order. The planning phase is where you catch impossible timelines, identify who is blocking whom, and decide in what sequence tasks must happen.

The first step in project planning is Work Breakdown Structure — splitting the project into phases, then splitting each phase into individual tasks. A task should be specific enough that one person can own it and it has a clear 'done' state. 'Build the website' is not a task. 'Create and test the Contact page in WordPress with form submission confirmation' is a task.

Once tasks are broken down, you assign durations, owners, and dependencies. A dependency means Task B cannot start until Task A is finished. Mapping dependencies reveals your critical path — the sequence of tasks that determines the absolute minimum time the project can take. Any delay on the critical path delays the whole project.

items.definition: A hierarchical decomposition of the project into phases, tasks, and sub-tasks. Every deliverable in the project should trace back to a WBS item.
A bar chart that maps project tasks against time. Each bar shows a task's start date, duration, and end date. Dependencies between tasks are shown with arrows.
A relationship between tasks where one task cannot begin until another is complete. For example: wireframes must be approved before development can start.
The longest sequence of dependent tasks in a project. Any delay to a task on the critical path delays the project completion date by the same amount.
A zero-duration checkpoint marking the completion of a significant phase or deliverable — for example, 'Client approves final wireframes' or 'Website goes live'.
A document listing identified project risks, their likelihood, potential impact, and the mitigation strategy the team will use if they occur.
Deliberately unallocated time built into a schedule to absorb unexpected delays. Also called 'contingency.' Rory's rule: 20% of estimated task duration, minimum.

blocks.text: Building a Gantt Chart

Gantt charts are the universal language of project delivery. Every project manager, every client, and every team lead can read one. They show at a glance whether the project is on track, who is overloaded, and which tasks are blocking everything else.

rows: Duration of each task as a horizontal bar across dates
Instantly shows how long each piece of work takes
Which tasks must finish before others can begin
Reveals the critical path and sequencing risks
Key checkpoints with no duration — just a date
Keeps the client and team aligned on major moments
Prevents double-booking and shows team workload balance
A vertical line showing the current date
Makes slippage immediately visible to everyone
Enables quick visual scanning of project health

blocks.title: Build a Gantt Chart in Trello
blocks.intro: You're building the project plan for the Bunbury Youth Theatre project. Use Trello's Timeline view to create a Gantt chart covering the full project from brief to delivery.
steps: Open Trello and create a new board titled 'Bunbury Youth Theatre — Digital Project'.
Create four lists: Backlog, In Progress, In Review, Done.

Add cards for at least 12 tasks covering: brief analysis, wireframes, design mockup, content writing, website development (split into at least 3 pages), video script, video shoot, video edit, final review, client approval, and launch.
For each card, add a start date and due date using Trello's date feature.
Assign each card to a team member (use yourself, Leo, Sparky, or Sloane).
Switch to Timeline view and check that the bars display correctly across your project dates.
Add a dependency between the wireframes card and the development cards — development cannot start until wireframes are approved.
Identify and add a label 'Critical Path' to the tasks that form your project's critical path.
Take a screenshot of your completed Timeline view and paste it into your project documentation.

blocks.text: Managing Risk Before It Becomes a Problem

Every project has risks. The question is whether you've identified them before they hit or whether you're scrambling to fix them mid-sprint. A risk register is not a pessimism exercise — it is a professional habit that separates teams that ship from teams that apologise.

rows: Build 3-day review window into schedule; chase 48hrs before deadline
Video shoot cancelled due to weather
Schedule indoor backup location; build 1-week float into post-production
Leo is sick during design sprint
Maintain shared Figma file with documented design decisions
Test on staging server before pushing to live; keep backup of last stable version
Reference scope statement; log requests in backlog for Phase 2 quote

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: I never start a project without a risk register, and the first item is always 'client communication breakdown.' Ninety percent of project failures I've seen come down to one thing: the team assumed the client understood something they didn't, or the client assumed the team would do something they weren't planning to. Document everything. If it isn't written down, it didn't happen. That's not cynicism — that's professional self-protection.

blocks.title: Scenario: The Immovable Deadline
blocks.scenario: The Bunbury Youth Theatre project must launch at the Winter Arts Festival on 20 July — that date cannot move. It is currently 1 June. Leo estimates design will take 2 weeks. Development will take 3 weeks. Video production needs 2 weeks for shooting and editing. Client review and revisions typically take 1 week per round and the client wants two rounds of review. There is also 1 week needed for final testing and deployment. Each phase has a dependency on the one before it.

questions.question: Calculate the total weeks required if all phases run sequentially. Does the project fit within the available time? Show your working.
Identify TWO phases that could potentially run in parallel (concurrently) to reduce the total timeline. Explain any risks created by running them at the same time.
Propose a revised project schedule that fits within the 20 July deadline. Present it as a table with phase, duration, start date, and end date. Explain any compromises you made.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · CRITICAL PATH SPRINT
blocks.text: Rory has given you a project with 10 tasks and their dependencies listed on a worksheet. Your challenge: draw the network diagram by hand (or use a diagramming tool), identify the critical path, and calculate the earliest completion date. Then answer: if Task 6 is delayed by 3 days, does it affect the final delivery date? Show your reasoning. Bring your answer to the next lesson.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Your project plan is only approved if:

✅ Gantt chart covers the full project from brief to delivery — no phases are missing
✅ Every task has an owner, a start date, and an end date
✅ At least three dependencies are mapped and visible
✅ The critical path is identified and labelled
✅ Risk register has at least five risks with likelihood, impact, and mitigation for each
✅ Buffer time (minimum 20% of total duration) is built into the schedule

Submit your Trello board link and risk register doc together. One without the other is an incomplete submission.

question.text: Which of the following BEST describes the critical path in a project?
options: The list of all tasks assigned to the most experienced team member
The shortest possible route through the project network diagram
The longest sequence of dependent tasks that determines minimum project duration
The tasks that have the highest risk rating in the risk register
question.explanation: The critical path is the longest chain of dependent tasks. Any delay to a task on this path directly extends the project end date. Tasks NOT on the critical path have 'float' — they can be delayed slightly without affecting the overall deadline.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4003 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 3 | Assigned to: Junior Project Coordinator

Leo needs sign-off on the design direction before a single pixel is placed in Figma. The client cannot read a colour palette and font sheet and tell you if they like it — they need to SEE the product. Your job this sprint: produce the design documentation. We need a set of wireframes for the website, a storyboard for the promo video, and an annotated mockup for the key landing page.

These documents are communication tools. They are how the client approves the direction before we invest weeks in development. A wireframe is not art — it is a contract. Produce them correctly, or Leo builds the wrong thing.
Why We Represent Designs Before We Build Them

Design representation techniques are tools for communicating ideas before committing to the full build. They let the team, the client, and stakeholders align on structure, content, and layout without spending time on details that might change. Getting approval on a wireframe takes an hour. Rebuilding a completed webpage takes a week.

There are three main levels of design fidelity: low fidelity (quick sketches and basic wireframes), medium fidelity (more detailed wireframes with real content), and high fidelity (polished mockups that closely resemble the final product). At Year 12 level, you must understand when each level is appropriate and be able to produce each type.

items.definition: A low-to-medium fidelity layout diagram showing the structure and content hierarchy of a screen or page. Uses boxes, lines, and placeholder text — no colour or final styling.
A high-fidelity static representation of a design showing the final visual appearance, including colour, typography, imagery, and spacing. Not interactive.
An interactive version of a design that simulates user interactions (clicks, navigation). Used for usability testing. Built in tools like Figma's prototype mode.
A sequence of illustrated frames showing the visual content, transitions, and audio/text elements of a video or animation in chronological order.
A design mockup or technical diagram with written notes (annotations) explaining design decisions, measurements, colours, fonts, or technical specifications.
The level of detail and polish in a design representation. Low fidelity = rough sketches; high fidelity = polished mockups that closely resemble the final product.

blocks.text: Wireframes: Structure Before Style

A wireframe answers one question: where does everything go? It shows navigation, content zones, headings, calls to action, and media placement — all without worrying about colour, fonts, or imagery. Wireframes force the conversation about structure and hierarchy before anyone gets attached to visual style.

Good wireframes are fast and functional. Use a grid, use real labels (not Lorem Ipsum for navigation items), and mark interactive elements clearly. Annotate anything that needs explanation — a 'search bar' box needs a note if it has autocomplete behaviour, for example. The wireframe should be readable by someone who has never seen the brief.

items.label: Do
points: Use real navigation labels — not 'Nav Item 1'
Show content hierarchy with clear heading levels
Label interactive elements (buttons, forms, dropdowns)
Annotate non-obvious interactions and behaviours
Include multiple screen sizes if responsive design is required
Number your frames and give each a clear page/screen name

items.label: Don't
points: Add colour — it distracts from structural feedback
Use 'Lorem Ipsum' for navigation or headings
Leave interactive elements unlabelled
Assume the client knows what a hamburger menu icon means
Create only one viewport (desktop only, ignoring mobile)
Skip annotations — unannotated wireframes create scope disputes later

blocks.title: Create Wireframes in Figma
blocks.intro: Leo has handed over the content outline for the Bunbury Youth Theatre website. Your job is to wireframe the Home page and the Shows page in Figma before any visual design begins.
steps: Open Figma and create a new file titled 'Bunbury Youth Theatre — Wireframes'.
Set up a frame at 1440px wide (desktop) for the Home page.
Add a navigation bar with the actual menu items from the brief: Home, Shows, About, Contact.
Add a hero section with a placeholder image box and a heading — use the actual heading text from the brief.
Add a 'Upcoming Shows' section with three show cards — each containing a title, date, and 'Book Now' button placeholder.
Add a footer with contact information and social media links.
Duplicate the frame and resize to 375px (mobile). Rearrange elements for a vertical mobile layout.
Add annotations using text notes to explain at least four design decisions (e.g. 'Hero image should be high-contrast for accessibility', 'Cards stack vertically on mobile').
Create a second frame for the Shows page with a list/grid of productions.
Export both frames as PNG files at 2x resolution.

blocks.text: Storyboards: Mapping Video and Animation

Storyboards are the wireframes of video and animation production. Before a camera is touched or a timeline is opened, a storyboard maps out every shot: what is visible, what the camera is doing, what audio plays, and what text appears on screen. A good storyboard eliminates surprises on shoot day and prevents expensive re-edits.

Each frame in a storyboard represents a shot or a scene transition. Frames include a rough illustration of the visual content, a shot description (wide shot, close-up, etc.), the dialogue or voiceover script, and the duration. You do not need to be an artist — stick figures and boxes communicate shot composition perfectly well.

rows: Rough sketch or diagram of the shot — show camera angle and subject position
Wide shot (WS), Medium shot (MS), Close-up (CU), Extreme close-up (ECU), Point of view (POV)
Static, pan left/right, tilt up/down, zoom in/out, tracking shot
Exact script text that plays during this frame
Any title cards, captions, or callouts that appear
Approximate length of this shot in seconds
Cut, dissolve, fade to black, wipe — how this shot ends

blocks.text: Annotated Diagrams: Explaining Design Decisions

An annotated diagram is a design mockup or technical diagram with written notes that explain the decisions behind each element. Annotations are not decorative — they provide the rationale, specifications, and implementation notes that a developer or content creator needs to build the design correctly.

Strong annotations reference design principles, client requirements, or accessibility standards. Weak annotations just name elements ('this is the logo'). Compare: 'Logo is placed top-left per F-pattern reading behaviour — maintains visual hierarchy' versus 'logo here.' One is professional documentation; the other is a label.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: I judge every intern's design documentation by one test: could a completely different developer pick up these wireframes and annotated mockups and build the right product without talking to anyone? If the answer is no, the documentation has failed its purpose. Design documentation is not for you — it is for the person who builds after you, reviews after you, or maintains the product in three years when you've moved on. Write for that person.

blocks.title: Scenario: The Unapproved Redesign
blocks.scenario: Leo produced wireframes for the Bunbury Youth Theatre website but skipped the annotation step because he was running behind schedule. The wireframes were approved by the client in a 15-minute meeting. During development, the developer interpreted the hero image placeholder as a static photograph. The client expected a video background with autoplay. The 'Book Now' button was built as a simple mailto: link; the client expected it to connect to their existing ticketing system API. Both issues required significant rework in the final week before launch.

questions.question: Explain how annotations on the wireframes would have prevented each of the two misunderstandings described. Be specific about what the annotations should have stated.

Describe what a client approval process for wireframes should include — beyond just showing the client the frames in a meeting — to ensure both parties have the same understanding of what will be built.
Identify the project management failure that allowed both issues to reach the development phase undetected. Suggest a process change that would catch similar issues earlier.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · STORYBOARD SPRINT
blocks.text: Rory needs a storyboard for a 60-second promotional video for the Bunbury Youth Theatre. The video should open with a teaser of a performance, introduce the theatre's story, show three upcoming productions, and close with the website URL and a call to action. Create a 10-frame storyboard using the template provided. Include shot types, camera movements, script text, and duration for every frame. You have 25 minutes. Rory is reviewing at the end of the lesson.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Your design documentation sprint is only approved if:

✅ Wireframes show both desktop and mobile layouts
✅ All navigation items use real labels from the brief — not placeholders
✅ Interactive elements (buttons, forms, links) are clearly labelled
✅ Annotations reference design decisions, not just element names
✅ Storyboard includes shot type, camera movement, script, and duration for every frame
✅ Annotated mockup shows at least five annotations with design rationale

Leo cannot start building from undocumented wireframes. Fix and resubmit.

question.text: Which type of design representation would be MOST appropriate for getting initial client approval of a website's page structure and navigation before any visual design work begins?
options: High-fidelity mockup with colour and typography
Interactive prototype with working navigation
Low-fidelity wireframe with annotations
Annotated screenshot of a competitor's website
question.explanation: Low-fidelity wireframes are ideal for early structural approval because they communicate layout and hierarchy without the distraction of visual style. Clients focus on structure rather than colour preferences, which is exactly what the team needs to agree on at this stage.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4004 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 4 | Assigned to: Junior Project Coordinator

We just had a near disaster. The social media content Leo designed for the Bunbury Youth Theatre was built for a Facebook audience — structured, information-heavy posts with long captions. The client's analytics show 78% of their audience is under 25 and predominantly on Instagram and TikTok. Leo's format would have bombed completely.

This is a reminder that who you are building for determines everything: format, tone, platform, file size, colour palette, typography, accessibility requirements. Your task this sprint: produce a target audience profile and a presentation medium recommendation report. These documents prevent us from building the right product for the wrong platform ever again.
Audience First, Medium Second

Every design decision in a digital product — from font size to file format to navigation structure — is determined by who the audience is and how they will access the product. Building without an audience profile is like Leo designing a poster without knowing if it will be printed on A4 paper or a 10-metre billboard.

At Year 12 level, understanding your audience means going beyond basic demographics. Age and location are a starting point. You also need to understand digital literacy, device preferences, connection speed, accessibility needs, cultural context, and the emotional state the user will be in when they encounter your product. Someone booking emergency flood assistance has very different needs from someone browsing tickets for a theatre show.

items.definition: The specific group of people a digital product is designed for, defined by characteristics including demographics, behaviours, needs, digital literacy, and access methods.
Measurable characteristics of an audience: age, gender, location, occupation, income, education level. Necessary but insufficient alone for audience analysis.
The values, interests, attitudes, and lifestyle characteristics of an audience. Answers 'what does this person care about?' rather than 'who are they statistically?'
The platform or format through which a digital product is delivered — for example, a website, printed flyer, social media post, kiosk screen, or email newsletter.
An audience member's ability to use and understand digital technology. Influences navigation complexity, vocabulary used in interface copy, and the level of instructional guidance needed.

A design constraint arising from an audience member's disability or limitation — for example, vision impairment requiring high contrast and large text, or motor impairment requiring keyboard navigation.

blocks.text: Audience Profiles: Beyond Demographics

An audience profile (sometimes called a persona) is a structured description of a representative member of your target audience. It goes beyond age and location to capture how this person uses technology, what they are trying to achieve, what frustrations they have, and what a successful experience looks like for them.

rows: Primary device, daily apps, online habits
Uses iPhone, Instagram daily, books everything via app
What are they trying to accomplish with this product?
Find show times and book tickets quickly — under 2 minutes
What frustrates them in similar products?
Hates websites that don't work on mobile; hates long forms
Confident, moderate, or low — and in what areas?
High on social/mobile apps; low on desktop form-based systems
Any visual, auditory, motor, or cognitive requirements?
None identified — but friend uses screen reader
When, where, and on what device will they use this?
On the bus, on her phone, in low-light conditions

blocks.text: Choosing the Right Presentation Medium

The presentation medium is where your audience encounters your product. The same content communicated through different mediums requires completely different design decisions. A promotional campaign targeting 16-year-olds in Bunbury should look nothing like one targeting 55-year-old corporate sponsors — even if the message is the same.

blocks.title: Medium Comparison: How Audience Determines Format

items.label: Website (Desktop-first)
points: Audience: professional adults researching organisations or making decisions
Navigation: multi-level menus acceptable; users expect to search and browse
Typography: body text 16–18px; longer reading time expected
File size tolerance: moderate — users on broadband at desk
Example: corporate sponsor information, grant applications

items.label: Mobile Website / PWA
points: Audience: younger users, casual interaction, on the move
Navigation: hamburger menu; maximum 3 taps to complete core task
Typography: body text 16px minimum; short paragraphs only
File size: strict — compress all images; avoid large video autoplay
Example: ticket booking, show times, social sharing

items.label: Social Media (Instagram/TikTok)
points: Audience: 15–30 predominantly; scrolling passively; low commitment
Format: vertical video (9:16), image carousels, short-form text
Content: hook in first 2 seconds; emotion-led; authentic tone
File size: platform-compressed — original export quality matters
Example: behind-the-scenes content, show highlights, cast announcements

items.label: Email Newsletter
points: Audience: opted-in subscribers — higher trust, willing to read more
Format: max 600px wide; inline CSS only; single-column recommended
Content: clear subject line; one primary CTA; plain text fallback
File size: email clients clip messages over 102KB (Gmail)
Example: season announcements, member updates, exclusive offers

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: The worst question you can ask about a presentation medium is 'what do we like?' The right question is 'where is our audience, what are they doing when they encounter us, and what do they need to be able to do in under 30 seconds?' Build for their context, not for your aesthetic preference. I've seen beautiful desktop websites built for audiences who are entirely on their phones. Beautiful — and completely useless.

blocks.title: Audience Profile and Medium Recommendation Report
blocks.intro: You're writing the audience analysis document for the Bunbury Youth Theatre project. This report will inform Leo's design decisions, the platforms we target, and the accessibility features we must include.
steps: Open Google Docs and create a document titled 'Audience Profile & Medium Recommendation — Bunbury Youth Theatre'.
Create two audience profiles: one for a 17-year-old student ticket buyer, one for a 50-year-old community sponsor/parent. Use the profile template structure from this lesson.
For each profile, identify THREE design implications — how this person's characteristics should influence specific design decisions.
Write a Medium Recommendation section with a table: Platform, Target Audience Segment, Content Format, Key Technical Constraints.
Include at least four recommended platforms with justification for each.
Add an Accessibility Considerations section listing at least three accessibility requirements and how each will be addressed in the design.
Write a short summary paragraph (max 4 sentences) explaining how the two audience profiles lead to different design priorities for the same project.

blocks.title: Scenario: Platform Mismatch
blocks.scenario: A Bunbury Youth Theatre grant application requires the organisation to demonstrate digital community engagement. The marketing intern has proposed: (a) a Facebook page updated twice weekly, (b) a monthly printed newsletter mailed to 200 addresses, (c) a static website with text-only content. The theatre's audience data shows 73% under 30, 89% access via mobile, and average session time under 90 seconds.

questions.question: Evaluate each of the three proposed platforms against the audience data provided. For each platform, explain whether it is appropriate, partially appropriate, or inappropriate — and why.

Propose an alternative or modified digital engagement strategy that better matches the audience profile. Include at least two specific platforms and justify each choice with reference to the audience data.

The printed newsletter targets a different audience segment (older community members and donors). Identify ONE specific design constraint created by the printed medium that would not apply to the digital platforms, and explain its impact.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · PERSONA SPRINT
blocks.text: Rory's new client is a regional community health service with two very different user groups: elderly residents (65+) managing medication schedules, and young parents (25–35) booking appointments. Your challenge: build one audience profile for each group in 15 minutes, then answer — should these two groups share the same website, or does the health service need two separate digital products? Present your recommendation with three reasons. Rory wants this on his desk in 20 minutes.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Your audience analysis is only approved if:

✅ Two distinct audience profiles are created — not just demographic tables, full profiles with goals, pain points, and digital literacy
✅ Each profile includes at least THREE specific design implications
✅ Medium recommendation table covers at least four platforms with technical constraints for each
✅ Accessibility requirements are addressed — not just mentioned
✅ Your summary paragraph connects audience profiles to specific design decisions

If Leo receives this document and can't tell what design decisions to make, the document has failed. Revise and resubmit.

question.text: A digital product for elderly community members (70+) requires different design decisions than one for teenagers. Identify TWO specific design decisions that would differ and explain why the audience characteristic drives each decision.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4005 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟠 IN REVIEW | Sprint 5 | Assigned to: Junior Project Coordinator

The Bunbury Youth Theatre website is built. The video is edited. Leo is happy with the design. Sparky says the server is stable. Sloane's content is loaded.

But we do not ship until it's been evaluated against the success criteria from the brief. Evaluation is not a formality — it is the step where we prove the product does what it was supposed to do, or we identify what to fix before the client sees it. I also need an iteration log: every change made after testing, with the reason recorded. This is how we demonstrate professional quality to the client — and it is how you demonstrate it in your assessment. Evaluation without evidence is just opinion.
Evaluation Is Not Proofreading

Evaluation is the systematic process of testing a digital product against defined criteria to determine whether it meets its purpose, audience needs, and technical requirements. It is not the same as checking for typos or making sure images load. It requires pre-defined criteria, structured testing methods, documented results, and evidence-based recommendations.

In professional practice, evaluation happens at multiple points in a project — not just at the end. Formative evaluation during development catches problems while they're cheap to fix. Summative evaluation at completion confirms the product meets all requirements before delivery. At Year 12, you must demonstrate both: evidence of feedback during the project AND a formal end-state evaluation.
